title: Human-Computer Interaction in Marine Research
description: A diagnostic branching course exploring HCI principles through marine biology applications. Your initial assessment determines whether you explore advanced research interfaces or master fundamental usability concepts.

nodes.title: HCI Fundamentals Diagnostic
nodes.content: This assessment evaluates your current understanding of Human-Computer Interaction principles. Your results will guide you to content matching your level.

questions.question: A marine biologist is designing a system to monitor coral reef health. The interface displays water temperature, pH, salinity, and species count in real-time. Which HCI principle is MOST important for this scenario?
options: A) The interface should use the most colorful design possible
B) The interface should match the mental model of how marine scientists conceptualize reef ecosystems
C) The interface should display as much data as possible in one view
D) The interface should only display information that is aesthetically pleasing
feedback: Incorrect. Colors help with aesthetics but aren't the core HCI principle here.
Correct! User-centered design means aligning the interface with how users think about their domain.
Incorrect. Cognitive overload reduces usability—users need relevant information organized meaningfully.
Incorrect. Function should drive design decisions, not aesthetics alone.
questions.explanation: In HCI, aligning the system's design with the user's mental model of their domain (in this case, how marine scientists understand reef ecosystems) is fundamental to creating usable interfaces.

questions.question: You're testing a new deep-sea research submersible's control interface with experienced pilots. A pilot says 'I keep looking for the sonar controls where the navigation buttons are.' What does this tell you?
options: A) The pilot is incompetent and needs more training
B) The interface layout violates the pilot's mental model of standard submarine controls
C) The pilot prefers a different color scheme
D) The submersible needs more powerful computers
feedback: Incorrect. User feedback about interface layout indicates a design issue, not user incompetence.
Correct! When users search for controls in unexpected places, it means the interface violates their expectations.
Incorrect. Color preferences are secondary to functional layout and discoverability.
Incorrect. Computing power doesn't affect interface logic or control placement.
questions.explanation: Usability testing reveals mismatches between interface design and user expectations. This feedback indicates the interface should be redesigned to match established conventions or the pilot's mental model.

questions.question: An aquarium's visitor information kiosk about fish species is poorly designed: visitors struggle to find information, abandon searches, and become frustrated. What is the PRIMARY value of user research?
options: A) It eliminates the need for design altogether
B) It discovers who the users are, what they need, and why they fail with the current design
C) It proves the designer was wrong
D) It guarantees the new design will be perfect
feedback: Incorrect. Design is always necessary—user research guides better design decisions.
Correct! User research uncovers the actual needs, behaviors, and pain points of your audience.
Incorrect. Research is collaborative and constructive, not about assigning blame.
Incorrect. Good design requires iteration; even research-informed designs need testing and refinement.
questions.explanation: User research is the foundation of Human-Centered Design. It helps you understand who your users are, what tasks they're trying to accomplish, and where the current design fails.

nodes.title: Advanced HCI: User-Centered Design for Complex Systems
nodes.content: Complex domains like deep-sea research demand interfaces that match users' sophisticated mental models. In HCI, this is called user-centered design: building systems around how experts actually think about their domain. Consider whale migration tracking systems where marine biologists must analyze movement patterns across years and multiple ocean regions. The interface must support their conceptual framework: seasonal patterns, population dynamics, geographic routes, and anomalies. Designers achieve this through iterative user research, prototyping with actual researchers, and designing information architecture that mirrors domain concepts. Accessibility and universal design are equally critical—interfaces must serve diverse researchers with different abilities and expertise levels.

nodes.title: Case Study: Deep-Sea Hydrothermal Vent Monitoring Interface
nodes.content: A research team at Woods Hole needed to monitor chemosynthetic ecosystems around hydrothermal vents. Traditional data dashboards showed raw sensor streams, but researchers struggled to detect ecosystem shifts. User research revealed researchers think in terms of 'vent stability'—a mental model combining temperature spikes, mineral deposition, and biological community changes. The redesigned interface organized data around this concept: a timeline view of vent stability with color-coded health indicators, a comparison tool for related vents, and alert systems for anomalies. By aligning the interface with the researchers' existing mental framework rather than forcing them to mentally combine raw sensor data, the team reduced analysis time by 40% and improved anomaly detection accuracy.

nodes.title: Course Complete: Advanced HCI Principles
nodes.content: Excellent work! You've mastered advanced HCI concepts including user-centered design for complex domains, mental model alignment, and accessibility considerations. These principles apply across marine research, medical systems, aerospace interfaces, and any complex field. Continue exploring how domain expertise, user research, and iterative design work together to create interfaces that truly serve their users' needs.

nodes.title: HCI Fundamentals: Usability and User Needs
nodes.content: Human-Computer Interaction is the study of how people interact with computing systems. Good HCI means designing interfaces that are easy to use and match what users actually need. Imagine a simple aquarium monitoring system where staff need to check water temperature, pH, and feeding schedules throughout the day. A usable interface shows these critical values clearly, uses consistent controls, and prevents errors like accidentally overfeeding. Poor HCI would hide crucial information, use confusing terminology, or require unnecessary steps. The foundation of HCI is understanding your users—who they are, what tasks they need to accomplish, and what they know. User research, usability testing, and iterative design are the core methods for creating interfaces that people can actually use effectively.

nodes.title: Example: Coral Reef Monitoring Dashboard
nodes.content: A marine sanctuary needed a system for staff to monitor coral health. The original design showed dozens of scientific measurements: pH, salinity, photosynthetically active radiation, zooxanthellae density, and more. Monitors overwhelmed staff who didn't know what numbers meant or how to spot problems. User research revealed staff needed simple answers: 'Is my reef healthy or stressed?' The redesigned dashboard uses a traffic-light system (green/yellow/red) based on raw measurements, complemented by simple explanations for each status. Staff could now quickly assess reef health, understand what was happening, and know when to contact scientists. This demonstrates the HCI principle that good design serves user tasks, not just displays available data.

nodes.title: Practice: Applying HCI Principles
nodes.content: Test your understanding of fundamental HCI concepts with these practice questions.

questions.question: You're designing a mobile app for whale watchers to identify whale species. Why is it important to research your actual users before designing?
options: A) User research guarantees the app will be perfect
B) You need to understand if users are experienced whale watchers or beginners, what identification features matter to them, and what tasks they're trying to accomplish
C) Research is only necessary for large, expensive projects
D) User research should replace the design process entirely
feedback: Incorrect. No design process guarantees perfection—iteration is essential.
Correct! Understanding your users' expertise level, needs, and tasks is fundamental to designing for them effectively.
Incorrect. User research is valuable for projects of any size.
Incorrect. User research informs and guides design; it doesn't replace the designer's work.
questions.explanation: User research reveals who your users are, what they're trying to do, and what they know. This knowledge is essential for making design decisions that actually serve their needs.

questions.question: An oceanographic research interface has several usability problems: buttons are tiny, labels use technical jargon, and users frequently make data entry errors. Which approach would BEST address these issues?
options: A) Make the interface prettier with better colors
B) Conduct usability testing with actual researchers to identify specific problems, then iteratively redesign and test improvements
C) Require all users to attend training courses
D) Replace the interface with a different software package
feedback: Incorrect. Aesthetics are secondary to functionality and usability.
Correct! Usability testing reveals what users struggle with; iterative design and retesting ensure improvements actually solve problems.
Incorrect. Training can't fix a poorly designed interface—good design should be learnable without extensive training.
Incorrect. Any new system would need the same user-centered design process.
questions.explanation: HCI emphasizes iterative design: design something, test it with users, identify problems, redesign, and test again. This is far more effective than guessing what will help.

questions.question: What does it mean to 'match the user's mental model' in interface design?
options: A) Make the interface look like the user's brain
B) Design the interface to match how users conceptually think about their domain and the tasks they're trying to accomplish
C) Force users to learn how computers work internally
D) Use psychological tests to pick interface colors
feedback: Incorrect. This is a metaphorical, not literal concept.
Correct! A user's mental model is their understanding of how something works. When interfaces match these frameworks, they're intuitive and learnable.
Incorrect. Users shouldn't need to understand computer internals—the interface should work the way they expect.
Incorrect. Mental models are about conceptual alignment, not color psychology.
questions.explanation: Mental models are how people understand their domain. A marine biologist has a mental model of how ecosystems work. An interface that aligns with this thinking (e.g., organizing data by ecosystem relationships rather than by sensor type) feels intuitive and supports effective work.

nodes.title: Course Complete: HCI Fundamentals
nodes.content: Great job! You've learned fundamental HCI principles including user research, usability, and matching user mental models. These concepts form the foundation for all interface design. Whether you're designing for marine research, business applications, or consumer products, remember: good design starts with understanding your users and iterates based on their feedback. Keep practicing these principles and you'll create interfaces that people genuinely want to use.
